narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 69.3% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction stems from a close look at key aspects of your academic record, like your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both sitting at the first decile, your LSAT score of 34, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, alongside your upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time. The two strongest factors pushing the model toward predicting bar failure were your low Year 3 decile ranking—far below the typical fifth or sixth decile seen among students who passed—and your similarly low Year 1 decile, both signaling early and sustained challenges in your school's grading distribution compared to successful bar takers. These stood out as the most influential, as they deviated sharply from the higher deciles that usually align with passing the bar. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA played a somewhat helpful role, pulling the prediction slightly toward success since your 3.00 is reasonably close to the 3.24 average among those who passed, softening the overall risk assessment a bit. Your LSAT score of 34, however, fell notably short of the 37.27 average for passers, adding to the concerns, though it wasn't the top driver here. Being full-time aligns well with the 93% of successful students in that category, and your upper-middle income bracket matches the most common group among passers at 45%, so those didn't weigh heavily against you. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by lagging most in those critical early-law grade deciles, where passers typically rank much higher, despite some strengths like your GPA and enrollment plans. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes to guide admissions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework if possible, and seeking targeted prep for law school grades based on your school's decile trends. You might also explore programs with strong bar support or part-time options if that fits better, and reapply after building these areas—many students turn predictions around with focused effort. Your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from an upper-middle background positions you well to improve these metrics and succeed next time.
